sentences.text: What's that name?
Fran?
And who's going to read this.
OK.
Else Dunner.
D-U-N-N-E-R.
Krieger.
K-R-I-E-G-E-R.
December [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 18, 1913.
In Bielitz-- B-I-E-L-I-T-Z-- A Polish Bielsko-- B-I-E-L-S-K-O.
Julius Krieger [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and Laura [NON-ENGLISH].
No, I was the only child.
He had the store.
We had the grocery store and then it changed to electrical supplies, like globes.
In town.
In Bielitz.
No.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not special.
Yeah.
More or less.
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was in the same house we lived, where we had the store.
It was friendly.
It was a friendly relationship.
Not close friends, but friends.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were friendly with all of them, because they were our customers, too.
No, I don't remember.
Quite a number, yes.
Oh, yes.
Yeah, there was-- there was one big synagogue, and other small synagogues, too.
Yes.
On holidays-- on high holidays and then holidays, yes.
How?

I-- we always observed it and we didn't go all the time to synagogue, but my mother's mother was very religious person, and she would never miss a holiday, and for having every thing strict kosher and everything.
Yeah.
Just a very nice building-- really nice shul-- it was like here-- Beth Israel.
It was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know-- conservative, I would say, because there was smaller places where they were Orthodox, strictly Orthodox.
But this was more conservative.
I don't know.
What do you mean by describing?
Yeah.
We were observant, as I said, but like on a Saturday, my father and mother, they had the store open, but my grandmother was strict shomer Shabbos, what they say.
And-- but [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she was very much observant.
And we kept a kosher house and everything to keep my grandmother happy.
Yeah.
That is-- that is a good question.
Friday night-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] There is a story about it-- I have a story about the Friday night-- and about the dream.
I-- one fine day, we were in Auschwitz already.
And I had the dream.
And I said to my cousins, with whom I was together-- I said, I woke up.
I was all upset and excited, and I said to her-- Tushka was her name-- Tushka, you'll see, we'll-- everything will be all right.
We don't need to worry.
We'll get out of here.
And she asked me, why, what I'm thinking?
And I told her that I had the dream.
And it showed me in the dream that I saw my father there, and I saw my grandmother and mother, and everything was ready for Friday evening.
The table was set, everything we did, like the candles were prepared to light, and everything, there And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And my father came in, and he was all dressed up for Friday evening.
And I just could see him with that golden chain like he used to wear.
And-- but he came in and he reached for his jacket and coat to go out.
And my mother said, what are you doing?
You know everything is set for Shabbos.
We-- you can't go now, because you never would sit down with my-- without my father for dinner.
And so, and then he wanted to leave.
And he said to her, and calmed her down, and he said, don't worry.
Everything will be all right.
I come home in time every single day.
And it upset me so much that I got, you know, and I saw the total picture.
And I said to my cousin, don't worry.
We will be looked after.
My father will look after.

And sure enough, maybe an hour later or so, it-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the-- the trumpet was calling, because usually when we had to go out and be counted or be there, then it was that we had-- that all the people had to go out.
And so we had to go out.
And the trumpet was there, and we had to go.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we had to go out.
And the same day, that was when we were counted again.
And we were counted by name-- there was a woman that came that Schindler had sent, and with a list of names.
And I was one of the-- on the list.
And they-- and-- and I were called, and we had to go.
They packed us into a train, and we went in the train, and on the same day we went out to Auschwitz.
Yeah.
Sure.
We had the Jewish school.
We had the Jewish school in Bielitz.
And I, of course, attended the Jewish school.
And when I was in-- up to grade five, but then we had to go to gymnasium-- to high school.
And we went to high school.
But there we had to go on Saturday-- there was school, too.
It wasn't a Jewish school.
But we-- but we had the-- we didn't have to write.
The people who were religious, they didn't have to write.
And then Saturday afternoon, we had sandwiches too in high school in synagogue for-- for the Jewish students.
Five minutes00 it was very close, like in the-- in the center.
It was close.
Yeah.
I walked because it was close.
But some walked and some didn't, depending how far they were away.
It was mixed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a mixed class.
Well, we had-- in town, we had the Maccabi, a group where-- and there was a man that was brought in even from Vienna.

And he was in-- he was looked after there-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] he-- how do you say-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I forgot now the name-- how you say it-- he looked after the activities-- after activities and gymnastic, and things like that.
Gymnastic.
We liked gymnastic.
Well, later, I mean there was all later, there was skiing.
And then I had some older cousins and they took me along, always.
Well, we had lessons twice a week where we went for gymnastic, twice a week.
And there was a place where we had all kinds of-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] well, we had quite a few where we could go and exercise, different exercises.
And then there was the time we had the men in the town who-- who-- who-- he was a builder.
He had a big company, and he helped us to build a chalet in the mountains.
So the students, the older students, they would go there in summer time and at the holiday, and they would even stay there, and helped to build the building to the house.
So--
It's where the Beskids, part of the Tatra.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] These where the Beskids, so-called.
What do you mean by special?
Well, I was very close with my father.
My father took me most of the time to-- to shows and to [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] the theatre, and things like that.
My father took me.
What do you mean by that?
No.
No.
It was a German, was a theatre.
Lots of things.
He was very, very educated.
He was very well educated and he was very respected in town.
And lots of people came to him for advice.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] like he was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he, too, he was a teacher.
He was a teacher, too, in Hebrew.
He was preparing, he-- in small towns, he would stay with families and-- and prepare the boys there for bar mitzvah.
And he learned it from his father, because that was his father's job.
But we had relatives-- we had relatives-- my grandmother had relatives who had-- who had a factory making all kinds of paper-- of paper things like for school.
And my grandmother wanted the boys to go there, because they were relatives, they should learn that job, and they should get a job there, and-- But my grandfather wouldn't let them.
Wouldn't let them because it wasn't good enough, just to be worker.
I-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know.
It's hard to say what I wanted.
I was very sports-minded, and I wanted to go skiing, and I wanted to do this-- and-- and this, I said, go in the mountains, and things like that.
Grade 12.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It was-- it just, kind of, grade 12.
There-- by that time, there was no school in German anymore.
So, but I wanted a degree, so I had to go to-- I got private lesson, and to go to Prague.
There was still a German-- in-- in German I could have the degree.
Pardon me?
I don't know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were in grade 12.
It would have been-- what age would I have been?
I don't remember.
I don't remember.
I just can't give you any number.
There was no German school anymore.
It was only in Polish.
And I didn't know Polish enough to have the exams.
That's why in there-- so that's why I got-- I had to go to-- to Prague and get exam.
And it helped me after because I had-- because I had [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the degree for the [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what that is, [NON-ENGLISH]?
The compensation.
They were paying compensation to people who-- who lost their jobs, or you didn't-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they couldn't work or they don't have enough experiences so.
So that helped me.
But that was after-- yeah--
Well, I was away with some my relatives.
I always went to my relatives.
My mother had a big family, and there was some children my age, so I went with them.
Well, I was there-- I was-- of course, we were very close family.
And they used to always to come, the ones that who were out of town, they would come to us, and I knew them close, so-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was-- it was nice.
I went there for summer holidays, and they had girls in my age, so I had a good time, because I'm, myself, was alone, so.
Sure.
Sure.
There was a difference between the Jews and the Gentiles.
Just like here, too, for the ones that are observing.
And they are not observing.
There was a difference.
No, we were very friendly with our neighbors, our Gentile neighbors.
But we knew that they were [INAUDIBLE], that there was a difference.
We didn't have Christmas, and we didn't-- and-- and they didn't have-- they didn't have Yom Kippur, and things like that.
So there--
Where?
In Prague?
I-- maybe I-- maybe I was in for a week, and after I had a cousin in Czechoslovakia, in [NON-ENGLISH], so I visited there for a week, and then I came home.
Well, I don't remember now exactly by the-- by the year or so, but my father passed away very early, and I had to take over the-- the-- the store and the bookkeeping, and everything.
When I was-- I was declared full of age with 18 years.

He had-- he was on a trip-- on a business trip-- and he-- and that's what we assume-- that he ate there something that didn't agree with him-- and they had to-- [NON-ENGLISH], something with the stomach, an upset stomach, and so-- and he got sick and he passed away, just like that.
What shall I tell you?
It was very hard, but-- but I had to take over.
And I still remember-- I'll never forget-- like I was doing my homework.
I was still in school.
And I was doing my homework.

And my mother came up and called me, and had tears in her eyes, and said, excuse us, if she's sorry that she has to call me but she can't help herself, and I have to come down and, you know, and work.
So I-- I had to take over.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And from then, I-- I did both.
My mother was very good.
She always wish-- she never would-- keeping me back from activities.
You know, like when I want to go for weekends, we went quite [INAUDIBLE].
Maccabi to-- to that chalet.
For that-- she-- I-- she never holded back.
I could always go.
Yeah.
Pardon me?
In--
Not, not at that time any-- not yet-- not that time yet.
But we knew that there's a difference between Jew and Gentile.
No.
No, I wouldn't-- no, I wouldn't say they way they were treating us, but as I say, we had-- didn't observe-- there were different observances, and different holidays, and we were aware of it.
Yeah.
